---
title: "W10-Dashboards Assignment"
subtitle: "Retail Seasonality Dashboard & Discount Effectiveness Explorer"
author: "Jake Evans"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    toc-expand: true
    number-sections: true
    embed-resources: true
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-overflow: wrap
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Overview

This report documents two dashboard deliverables created using the IBM 6540 group project retail sales dataset from Kaggle, titled *Retail Sales Data with Seasonal Trends & Marketing*. The dataset includes 30,000 transactions recorded between January 2022 and January 2024 across four product categories.

Project 1 is a static Quarto dashboard that presents a story focused on seasonal sales patterns. Project 2 is an interactive Shiny application that allows users to explore the effectiveness of discounts.

# Project 1: Seasonality & Timing Dashboard

## Project Description

This static dashboard is designed to answer one main question for a non-technical retail stakeholder: **when during the year do customers actually make purchases, and how much does timing matter?**

It highlights the peak sales month, the strongest day of the week, and the increase in revenue on holiday dates. These headline findings are supported by a monthly sales trend, a category by month heatmap, and a separate breakdown for each product category.

Because the goal is to communicate a clear, established finding rather than allow users to explore the data themselves, a static dashboard is the most appropriate format. Each chart and value box contributes to the same seasonal story, and no filters or additional interaction are needed to understand the main results.

## Data Description

The dataset contains 30,000 daily transaction-level records spanning January 1, 2022 to January 1, 2024, with the following fields:

| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| Store ID | Store identifier (single store in this dataset) |
| Product ID | Product identifier |
| Date | Transaction date |
| Units Sold | Number of units sold |
| Sales Revenue (USD) | Revenue generated |
| Discount Percentage | Discount applied (0-20%) |
| Marketing Spend (USD) | Marketing spend associated with the transaction |
| Store Location | Country associated with the transaction |
| Product Category | Clothing, Electronics, Furniture, or Groceries |
| Day of the Week | Day name |
| Holiday Effect | Boolean flag for whether the date was a holiday |

No missing values were found across any column. The dashboard uses Date, Sales Revenue, Product Category, Day of the Week, and Holiday Effect to build the seasonality story.

## R Code

This is the full source of `Evans,Jake-M10-Dashboard-Static.qmd`, shown here as plain text.

```` r
---
title: "Retail Seasonality & Timing Dashboard"
subtitle: "When Do Customers Buy? A Seasonal Analysis of Retail Sales"
author: "Jake Evans"
date: today
format:
  dashboard:
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
    orientation: columns
    nav-buttons:
      - icon: github
        href: https://github.com/jakevns/RStudio
        aria-label: GitHub repository
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```​{r}
#| label: setup

library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(scales)
library(gt)

sales <- read_csv("data/Retail_sales.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  rename(
    store_id         = `Store ID`,
    product_id       = `Product ID`,
    date             = Date,
    units_sold       = `Units Sold`,
    revenue          = `Sales Revenue (USD)`,
    discount_pct     = `Discount Percentage`,
    marketing_spend  = `Marketing Spend (USD)`,
    store_location   = `Store Location`,
    category         = `Product Category`,
    day_of_week      = `Day of the Week`,
    holiday          = `Holiday Effect`
  ) |>
  mutate(
    date        = as_date(date),
    month       = month(date, label = TRUE, abbr = TRUE),
    year        = year(date),
    day_of_week = factor(
      day_of_week,
      levels = c("Monday", "Tuesday", "Wednesday", "Thursday",
                 "Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday")
    )
  )

gold_green <- c("#1B4332", "#2C5F2D", "#52796F", "#84A98C", "#CAD2C5")
```

```​{r}
#| label: calcs

total_revenue <- sum(sales$revenue)

monthly_revenue <- sales |>
  group_by(month) |>
  summarise(total_revenue = sum(revenue), .groups = "drop")

peak_month <- monthly_revenue |>
  slice_max(total_revenue, n = 1) |>
  pull(month) |>
  as.character()

dow_revenue <- sales |>
  group_by(day_of_week) |>
  summarise(avg_revenue = mean(revenue), .groups = "drop")

best_day <- dow_revenue |>
  slice_max(avg_revenue, n = 1) |>
  pull(day_of_week) |>
  as.character()

holiday_compare <- sales |>
  group_by(holiday) |>
  summarise(avg_revenue = mean(revenue), .groups = "drop")

holiday_avg     <- holiday_compare |> filter(holiday == TRUE)  |> pull(avg_revenue)
non_holiday_avg <- holiday_compare |> filter(holiday == FALSE) |> pull(avg_revenue)
holiday_lift    <- (holiday_avg - non_holiday_avg) / non_holiday_avg * 100
```

# Overview

## Column {width="65%"}

### Row {height="25%"}

```​{r}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Total Revenue"
#| icon: cash-coin
#| color: primary
list(value = dollar(total_revenue, largest_with_cents = 0, scale = 1/1e6, suffix = "M"))
```

```​{r}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Peak Month"
#| icon: calendar-heart
#| color: success
list(value = peak_month)
```

```​{r}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Best Day of Week"
#| icon: star-fill
#| color: success
list(value = best_day)
```

```​{r}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Holiday Revenue Lift"
#| icon: gift-fill
#| color: "#84A98C"
list(value = paste0(round(holiday_lift, 1), "%"))
```

### Row {height="75%"}

```​{r}
#| title: "Monthly Revenue Trend"

monthly_trend <- sales |>
  mutate(year_month = floor_date(date, "month")) |>
  group_by(year_month) |>
  summarise(total_revenue = sum(revenue), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(monthly_trend, aes(x = year_month, y = total_revenue)) +
  geom_line(color = "#2C5F2D", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(color = "#2C5F2D", size = 2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_dollar(scale = 1/1000, suffix = "K")) +
  scale_x_date(date_labels = "%b %Y", date_breaks = "2 months") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Total Revenue") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

```​{r}
#| title: "Average Revenue by Product Category and Month"

cat_month <- sales |>
  group_by(category, month) |>
  summarise(avg_revenue = mean(revenue), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(cat_month, aes(x = month, y = category, fill = avg_revenue)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#EAF4EA", high = "#1B4332", labels = label_dollar()) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = "Avg Revenue") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

## Column {width="35%"}

### Row {height="55%"}

```​{r}
#| title: "Average Revenue by Day of Week"

ggplot(dow_revenue, aes(x = day_of_week, y = avg_revenue, fill = day_of_week)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = rep(gold_green, length.out = 7)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_dollar()) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Avg Revenue per Transaction") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

### Row {height="45%"}

```​{r}
#| title: "Holiday vs. Non-Holiday Revenue"

holiday_compare |>
  mutate(holiday = if_else(holiday, "Holiday", "Non-Holiday")) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(holiday = "Date Type", avg_revenue = "Avg Revenue") |>
  fmt_currency(columns = avg_revenue, decimals = 2) |>
  data_color(
    columns = avg_revenue,
    method = "numeric",
    palette = c("#EAF4EA", "#1B4332")
  ) |>
  tab_options(table.font.size = "0.85em")
```

# By Category

## Column {width="100%"}

### Row {height="60%"}

::: {.card title="Monthly Revenue Trend by Category"}

Each line below tracks total monthly revenue for one product category, letting you compare how seasonality plays out differently across Clothing, Electronics, Furniture, and Groceries.

```​{r}
cat_trend <- sales |>
  mutate(year_month = floor_date(date, "month")) |>
  group_by(category, year_month) |>
  summarise(total_revenue = sum(revenue), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(cat_trend, aes(x = year_month, y = total_revenue, color = category)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = gold_green[1:4]) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_dollar(scale = 1/1000, suffix = "K")) +
  scale_x_date(date_labels = "%b %Y", date_breaks = "3 months") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Total Revenue", color = "Category") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

:::

### Row {height="40%"}

```​{r}
#| title: "Category Summary Statistics"

sales |>
  group_by(category) |>
  summarise(
    total_revenue   = sum(revenue),
    avg_units_sold  = mean(units_sold),
    avg_discount    = mean(discount_pct),
    holiday_share   = mean(holiday) * 100,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(total_revenue)) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    category       = "Category",
    total_revenue  = "Total Revenue",
    avg_units_sold = "Avg Units Sold",
    avg_discount   = "Avg Discount %",
    holiday_share  = "% Sold on Holidays"
  ) |>
  fmt_currency(columns = total_revenue, decimals = 0) |>
  fmt_number(columns = avg_units_sold, decimals = 1) |>
  fmt_number(columns = avg_discount, decimals = 1, pattern = "{x}%") |>
  fmt_number(columns = holiday_share, decimals = 1, pattern = "{x}%") |>
  data_color(
    columns = total_revenue,
    method = "numeric",
    palette = c("#EAF4EA", "#1B4332")
  ) |>
  tab_options(table.font.size = "0.9em")
```
````

## Findings from the Data

* **Total revenue:** The headline value box summarizes total revenue across the full two-year period. This gives the audience an immediate sense of scale before they look more closely at the seasonal patterns.

* **Peak month:** The value box identifies the month with the highest revenue, making it easy to see whether sales are concentrated around a particular season, such as the holiday shopping period, or remain relatively consistent throughout the year.

* **Best day of the week:** The dashboard highlights the day with the highest average revenue. This finding can help support decisions related to staffing, campaign timing, and promotional scheduling.

* **Holiday revenue lift:** The dashboard compares average revenue on holiday dates with average revenue on non-holiday dates. This helps show whether holidays have a meaningful effect on sales. A positive lift may support increasing marketing activity around holiday periods, while little or no lift would suggest that the “Holiday Effect” variable is not a strong driver in this dataset.

* **Category seasonality differs:** The monthly trend lines on the “By Category” page show that product categories do not all peak at the same time. This suggests that category-specific promotional calendars may be more effective than using one seasonal strategy across the entire business.


## Interpret the Dashboard Charts

* The **monthly revenue trend line** on the Overview page shows the overall pattern of demand across the two-year period. This makes it easier to see whether revenue is increasing, following a seasonal pattern, or remaining relatively stable.

* The **category-by-month heatmap** summarizes four product categories across twelve months in a single visual. Darker tiles represent higher average revenue, allowing the reader to quickly identify the category and month combinations that stand out most.

* The **day-of-week bar chart** compares average revenue per transaction across each weekday. It provides a clear answer to whether certain days may be better suited for promotions or other sales activity.

* The **holiday versus non-holiday table** places both averages side by side and uses color shading to make the difference easy to see. This helps the reader understand the size of the holiday effect without needing to calculate it manually.

* The **By Category page** examines the same seasonal question at the individual category level. It includes both trend lines and a summary table showing total revenue, average units sold, average discount, and the share of holiday sales for each category, making direct comparisons easier.

## Clickable URL of the Published Web Page

[https://jakevns.github.io/RStudio/M10/Evans,Jake-M10-Dashboard-Static.html](https://jakevns.github.io/RStudio/M10/Evans,Jake-M10-Dashboard-Static.html)

# Project 2: Discount Effectiveness Explorer (Shiny App)

## Project Description

This interactive Shiny application focuses on a different question from Project 1: **does discounting lead to higher unit sales and revenue, and if so, by how much?**

Instead of presenting one fixed conclusion, the application is designed for exploration. A marketing stakeholder can filter the data by product category, discount level, date range, and holiday status to see how the relationship between discounts and sales changes across different conditions.

This creates a clearly different story from the seasonality dashboard. Project 1 examines *when* customers are most likely to buy, while this application explores *what factors may encourage them to buy more*.

## Data Description

The application uses the same 30,000-row retail sales dataset described in Project 1. For the summary table, discount values are grouped into three tiers based on the dataset’s actual discount range of 0% to 20%: **Low (0–5%)**, **Moderate (6–12%)**, and **High (13–20%)**.


## R Code

This is the full source of `discount-explorer/app.R`, shown here as plain text.

```r
library(shiny)
library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(plotly)
library(DT)
library(scales)

# ---- Data prep (loaded once at app start, not reactive) ----

sales <- read_csv("Retail_sales.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  rename(
    store_id         = `Store ID`,
    product_id       = `Product ID`,
    date             = Date,
    units_sold       = `Units Sold`,
    revenue          = `Sales Revenue (USD)`,
    discount_pct     = `Discount Percentage`,
    marketing_spend  = `Marketing Spend (USD)`,
    store_location   = `Store Location`,
    category         = `Product Category`,
    day_of_week      = `Day of the Week`,
    holiday          = `Holiday Effect`
  ) |>
  mutate(
    date = as_date(date),
    discount_tier = case_when(
      discount_pct <= 5  ~ "Low (0-5%)",
      discount_pct <= 12 ~ "Moderate (6-12%)",
      TRUE               ~ "High (13-20%)"
    ),
    discount_tier = factor(
      discount_tier,
      levels = c("Low (0-5%)", "Moderate (6-12%)", "High (13-20%)")
    )
  )

categories <- sort(unique(sales$category))
min_date   <- min(sales$date)
max_date   <- max(sales$date)

# ---- UI ----

ui <- fluidPage(
  titlePanel("Discount Effectiveness Explorer"),
  p("Does discounting actually move units and revenue? Filter by category, ",
    "discount range, and date window below to explore the relationship ",
    "between discount depth and sales performance."),

  sidebarLayout(
    sidebarPanel(
      checkboxGroupInput(
        "category", "Product Category",
        choices  = categories,
        selected = categories
      ),
      sliderInput(
        "discount_range", "Discount Percentage Range",
        min = 0, max = 20, value = c(0, 20), step = 1, post = "%"
      ),
      dateRangeInput(
        "date_range", "Date Range",
        start = min_date, end = max_date,
        min   = min_date, max = max_date
      ),
      checkboxInput("holiday_only", "Holiday dates only", value = FALSE),
      hr(),
      helpText("Discount tiers used in the summary table below: ",
               "Low = 0-5%, Moderate = 6-12%, High = 13-20%.")
    ),

    mainPanel(
      fluidRow(
        column(3, uiOutput("box_transactions")),
        column(3, uiOutput("box_avg_units")),
        column(3, uiOutput("box_avg_revenue")),
        column(3, uiOutput("box_avg_discount"))
      ),
      br(),
      plotlyOutput("scatter_plot", height = "350px"),
      br(),
      plotlyOutput("trend_plot", height = "300px"),
      br(),
      h4("Summary by Discount Tier"),
      DTOutput("tier_table")
    )
  )
)

# ---- Server ----

server <- function(input, output, session) {

  filtered <- reactive({
    req(input$category, input$discount_range, input$date_range)

    df <- sales |>
      filter(
        category     %in% input$category,
        discount_pct >= input$discount_range[1],
        discount_pct <= input$discount_range[2],
        date         >= input$date_range[1],
        date         <= input$date_range[2]
      )

    if (input$holiday_only) {
      df <- df |> filter(holiday == TRUE)
    }

    df
  })

  value_box <- function(value, label) {
    div(
      style = "text-align:center; background:#EAF4EA; border-radius:8px; padding:12px; margin-bottom:10px;",
      h3(style = "margin:0; color:#1B4332;", value),
      p(style = "margin:0; color:#52796F;", label)
    )
  }

  output$box_transactions <- renderUI({
    value_box(comma(nrow(filtered())), "Transactions")
  })

  output$box_avg_units <- renderUI({
    df <- filtered()
    val <- if (nrow(df) == 0) "—" else round(mean(df$units_sold), 1)
    value_box(val, "Avg Units Sold")
  })

  output$box_avg_revenue <- renderUI({
    df <- filtered()
    val <- if (nrow(df) == 0) "—" else dollar(mean(df$revenue))
    value_box(val, "Avg Revenue")
  })

  output$box_avg_discount <- renderUI({
    df <- filtered()
    val <- if (nrow(df) == 0) "—" else paste0(round(mean(df$discount_pct), 1), "%")
    value_box(val, "Avg Discount")
  })

  output$scatter_plot <- renderPlotly({
    df <- filtered()
    validate(need(nrow(df) > 0, "No data for the selected filters."))

    plot_df <- df |>
      group_by(discount_pct, category) |>
      summarise(avg_units = mean(units_sold), .groups = "drop")

    p <- ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = discount_pct, y = avg_units, color = category)) +
      geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.7) +
      geom_smooth(aes(group = category), method = "lm", se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.7) +
      scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
      labs(
        x = "Discount Percentage", y = "Avg Units Sold", color = "Category",
        title = "Discount Level vs. Average Units Sold"
      ) +
      theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

    ggplotly(p)
  })

  output$trend_plot <- renderPlotly({
    df <- filtered()
    validate(need(nrow(df) > 0, "No data for the selected filters."))

    plot_df <- df |>
      mutate(year_month = floor_date(date, "month")) |>
      group_by(year_month) |>
      summarise(total_revenue = sum(revenue), .groups = "drop")

    p <- ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = year_month, y = total_revenue)) +
      geom_line(color = "#2C5F2D", linewidth = 1) +
      geom_point(color = "#2C5F2D", size = 1.5) +
      scale_y_continuous(labels = label_dollar(scale = 1/1000, suffix = "K")) +
      labs(x = NULL, y = "Total Revenue", title = "Revenue Over Time (Filtered)") +
      theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

    ggplotly(p)
  })

  output$tier_table <- renderDT({
    df <- filtered()
    validate(need(nrow(df) > 0, "No data for the selected filters."))

    df |>
      group_by(discount_tier) |>
      summarise(
        transactions   = n(),
        avg_units_sold = round(mean(units_sold), 1),
        avg_revenue    = round(mean(revenue), 2),
        total_revenue  = round(sum(revenue), 2),
        .groups = "drop"
      ) |>
      rename(
        `Discount Tier`   = discount_tier,
        `Transactions`    = transactions,
        `Avg Units Sold`  = avg_units_sold,
        `Avg Revenue`     = avg_revenue,
        `Total Revenue`   = total_revenue
      ) |>
      datatable(rownames = FALSE, options = list(dom = "t"))
  })
}

shinyApp(ui, server)
```

## Key Findings from the Data

* **Discount depth versus units sold:** The scatter plot and category-level trend lines show whether larger discounts are associated with higher unit sales. They also make it possible to compare whether this relationship is consistent across all four categories or stronger in categories such as Clothing and Electronics than in Groceries.

* **Discount tier summary table:** The summary table compares average units sold and average revenue across the Low, Moderate, and High discount tiers. This helps show whether deeper discounts increase revenue per transaction or whether the additional units sold are not enough to offset the lower selling price. This is an important consideration when evaluating whether aggressive discounting is worth the potential reduction in margin.

* **Holiday-only filtering:** The holiday filter allows users to isolate holiday dates and examine whether the relationship between discounts and sales changes during those periods. This can help determine whether holiday promotions may benefit from deeper discounts or whether a different strategy would be more effective.

* **Date range filtering:** The date range filter allows users to examine whether the effect of discounting remained consistent throughout the two-year period or changed over time.


## Interpret the Dashboard Charts

* The four **summary boxes** at the top of the application show the number of transactions, average units sold, average revenue, and average discount. Each value updates automatically when the filters change, giving users a quick overview of the selected data before they examine the charts.

* The **discount versus units sold scatter plot** is the main exploratory visual in the application. Each point represents the average units sold at a specific discount level and is colored by product category. A separate linear trend line is included for each category, making the overall direction and relative strength of the discount relationship easier to see.

* The **revenue-over-time line chart** displays total revenue for the current filter selection across the two-year period. This allows users to examine whether the results of a discount strategy appear consistent over time or vary with seasonal demand.

* The **discount tier table** provides the most accessible summary for non-technical stakeholders. It groups the continuous discount variable into three clear tiers and reports the number of transactions, average units sold, average revenue, and total revenue for each group. This makes the tradeoff between discount depth and sales performance easier to understand without requiring a statistical background.


## Interactive App

The Shiny app should be deployed to shinyapps.io using the `app.R` and `Retail_sales.csv` files in the `discount-explorer/` folder (via the RStudio **Publish** button or `rsconnect::deployApp()`).

## Clickable URL of the Shiny App

[https://o7gye8-jake-evans.shinyapps.io/discount-explorer/](https://o7gye8-jake-evans.shinyapps.io/discount-explorer/)

*A live version of this report is also available on [GitHub Pages](https://jakevns.github.io/RStudio/M10/Evans,Jake-M10-Dashboards.html).*
